EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drier air began to work into eastern Utah in the wake of a recent monsoon surge. Even so, enough residual moisture was present to fuel isolated thunderstorms. Some storms produced wind gusts in excess of 58 mph in Grand County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A peak wind gust of 60 MPH was measured by the automated weather station at the Canyonlands Field Airport outside of Moab.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moisture increased across eastern Utah ahead of the next Pacific system. This, paired with daytime heating, led to scattered showers. Strong outflow from collapsing showers produced gusty winds in excess of 58 mph across portions of southeast Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A peak wind gust of 61 MPH was measured by the automated weather station at the Blanding Municipal Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Pacific trough dug into the Great Basin as the upper level jet rounding its base advected abundant moisture into the Four Corners Region. This resulted in moderate to heavy snow across most of the mountain ranges across eastern Utah as well as some valleys.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An estimated 18 to 26 inches of snow fell across the Abajo Mountains with lesser amounts of 4 to 8 inches over the La Sals.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Pacific trough dug into the Great Basin as the upper level jet rounding its base advected abundant moisture into the Four Corners Region. This resulted in moderate to heavy snow across most of the mountain ranges across eastern Utah as well as some valleys.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 3 inches of snow fell in and around the Blanding area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An atmospheric river brought an abundance of moisture to Eastern Utah. This, paired with the passage of an upper level trough across the Northern Rockies and associated cold front, resulted in moderate to heavy snow for the northern and southern mountains. Mild air associated with the atmospheric river kept snow levels high with mostly all rain or a rain/snow mix in the valleys. Winds gusted up to 58 mph at times in the mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 6 to 12 inches of snow fell across the area. Winds gusted up to 45 mph at times.

Mild air associated with the atmospheric river kept snow levels high with mostly all rain or a rain/snow mix in the valleys. Winds gusted up to 58 mph at times in the mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 5 to 9 inches of snow fell across the area with locally higher amounts of up to 16 inches estimated at the Mosby Mountain SNOTEL site. Much of the snow fell in 12 hours or less. Winds gusted up to 58 mph at times.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Pacific trough dug into the Great Basin as the upper level jet rounding its base advected abundant moisture into the Four Corners Region. This resulted in moderate to heavy snow across most of the mountain ranges across eastern Utah as well as some valleys.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An estimated 6 inches of snow fell at the East Willow Creek SNOTEL site.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moisture from a decaying atmospheric river event paired with the passage of a strong cold front resulted in moderate to heavy snow across much of eastern Utah. In addition, gusty winds led to significantly reduced visibility in blowing and drifting snow. Winds gusted up to 58 mph at times in the valleys, and were stronger than 70 mph in some mountain areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 10 to 20 inches of snow fell across the area with locally higher amounts of up to 22 inches estimated at the Kings Cabin and Trout Creek SNOTEL sites. Winds gusted up to 47 mph at times.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: An estimated 2 feet of snow fell at the East Willow Creek SNOTEL site. Winds gusted up to 56 mph at times.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low level moisture remained trapped across eastern Utah. This led to the development of dense fog across the Eastern Uinta Basin.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dense fog was observed across the Eastern Uinta Basin including at the Vernal Regional Airport as visibility was reduced to less than one quarter of a mile for several hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Winds increased as yet another upper level trough and associated cold front dropped into the Great Basin. This led to some stronger winds across eastern Utah. Some stronger gusts met high wind criteria, or 75 mph, in the Eastern Uinta Mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A peak wind gust of 78 MPH was measured by the automated weather station at Little Mountain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An abnormally strong and cold low pressure system dropped into the Great Basin and lifted across the Western Slope. Ample cold air and moisture paired with favorable orographics resulted in moderate to heavy snow for most of the mountains and valleys across eastern Utah. Additionally, winds gusts exceeded 30 mph in the some valleys at times.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 8 to 16 inches of snow fell across the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unsettled weather pattern persisted during much of the month of February. This resulted in above normal precipitation for portions of eastern Utah. This allowed the drought conditions to improve in some areas, with all evidence of extreme (D3) drought being removed from the region. Elsewhere, conditions remained relatively unchanged through the end of the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The status of the drought improved across portions of the area through the month of February. The extreme (D3) drought across the far western border of the zone improved to the moderate (D1) category. Moderate to abnormally dry (D0) drought persisted elsewhere through the end of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The first in a series of shortwave disturbances tracked across eastern Utah. Heavy snow fell across the La Sal and Abajo Mountains in southeast Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 12 to 18 inches of snow fell across the area with locally higher amounts in excess of 2 feet estimated at the Buckboard Flat SNOTEL site. Up to 10 inches of snow fell in Monticello.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another atmospheric river event impacted eastern Utah resulting in widespread showers across the region. The southwest flow ahead of the system resulted in favorable upslope conditions for the southern mountains where moderate to heavy snow fell. The other mountain ranges saw moderate snowfall with this event as well as in the Eastern Uinta Basin. Winds gusted close to 50 mph at times in the higher elevations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 9 to 18 inches of snow fell across the area. Winds gusted close to 50 mph at times.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A midlevel low pressure system dove into far southwest Utah and northern Arizona. Wraparound moisture associated with this low moved into southeast Utah. This resulted in moderate snow across the Abajo Mountains in a short period.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 6 to 12 inches of snow fell across the Abajo Mountains.

CZ_NAME: GREAT SALT LAKE DESERT AND MOUNTAINS
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another winter storm moved through the region, prompting Winter Storm Warnings for almost all of Utah's mountains, and Winter Weather Advisories for many northern Utah Valleys for snowfall accumulation.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several high elevation SNOTEL stations (above 7000 feet) including Vernon Creek, Rocky Basin-Settlement, and Mining Fork recorded 1.2 to 1.9 inches of snow water equivalent, resulting in approximately 19 to 27 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another system in an active period resulted in periods of moderate to heavy rainfall across southern Utah, resulting in isolated flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Persistent moderate to at times heavy rain resulted in flash flooding throughout Beaver Dam Wash. For example, the Catclaw Canyon stream gage increased from a dry no-flow state of 0 CFS to a peak flow of approximately 2923 CFS. Cameras also showed water flowing in this typically dry wash.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm brought an initial surge of widespread snow from January 9 into January 10, with a second bout of snow later January 10 on into January 11. Occasional strong wind gusts were also noted throughout the event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A combination of Coop observations and mesonet observations at locations including Kamas, Snyderville, and Liberty indicated 1.1 to 3.5 inches of snow water equivalent, resulting in approximately 8 to 22 inches of snow.

CZ_NAME: GREAT SALT LAKE DESERT AND MOUNTAINS
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another prolonged and unsettled pattern took hold in the middle of January, resulting in widespread precipitation and moderate high elevation snow accumulation, with some more modest amounts noted at lower elevations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several high elevation SNOTEL stations (above 7000 feet) including Dry Fork, George Creek, and Rocky Basin Settlement recorded 1.5 to 3.1 inches of snow water equivalent, resulting in approximately 22 to 56 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of weak disturbances resulted in light to moderate snow accumulations for much of the region to close out the month, with the highest amounts noted in the higher terrain. Additionally, the storm brought a period of anomalous cold as it departed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several SNOTEL stations including Thaynes Canyon, Brighton, and Mill-d North recorded 0.9 to 1.7 inches of snow water equivalent, resulting in approximately 14 to 27 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A second shortwave disturbance tracked across eastern Utah. Favorable orographics in the northwest flow resulted in heavy snow across the Eastern Uinta Mountains with lesser amounts over the Tavaputs Plateau. Winds gusted up to 47 mph at times.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 10 to 20 inches of snow fell across the area. Winds gusted up to 47 mph at times.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A snowfall accumulation of 6.5 inches was observed at Hildale. An accumulation of 5.5 inches with 0.52 inches of snow water equivalent was observed at Kanab.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Abundant monsoon moisture resulted in widespread showers and thunderstorms across eastern Utah. Even with a well-saturated profile, the stronger thunderstorms produced gusty winds in excess of 58 mph in addition to heavy rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A peak wind gust of 60 MPH was measured by the automated weather station at the Canyonlands Field Airport outside of Moab.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Snow was observed for much of Utah, with more than a foot of accumulation in portions of the Cottonwoods.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A snowfall accumulation of 20 inches was observed at the Alta-Collins mesonet site at Alta Ski Area, with 1.07 inches of snow water equivalent recorded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Valley snow accumulations of near 12 inches occurred across portions of southern Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A snowfall accumulation of 10.8 inches with 0.79 inches of snow water equivalent was observed at Cedar City. A snowfall accumulation of 6.0 inches was observed at Enoch.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A snowfall accumulation of 10.0 inches with 0.45 inches of snow water equivalent was observed at Bullfrog.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wind gust of 76 mph was recorded at the White Reef mesonet site and the St George Regional Airport AWOS recorded a peak wind gust of 63 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Abnormally cold temperatures filtered into Eastern Utah in the wake of a strong cold front. This led to subfreezing temperatures across some of the southeast valleys where vegetation was susceptible to damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Overnight lows ranged from 27 to 32 degrees across the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Abnormally cold temperatures filtered into Eastern Utah in the wake of a strong cold front. This led to subfreezing temperatures across some of the southeast valleys where vegetation was susceptible to damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Overnight lows ranged from 25 to 29 degrees across the area, including a low of 27 degrees at the Canyonlands Field Airport in Moab.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Valley snow accumulations of near 12 inches occurred across portions of southern Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow squall. A snow squall that pushed across Interstate 15 in southwest Utah resulted in an 11-vehicle accident Tuesday. Both northbound lanes of Interstate 15 near milepost 52 were closed. Numerous road closures from snow were reported, including state Route 9 from Zion National Park eastward through the intersection with U.S. Highway 89.  The closure was from 4:20 PM Tuesday to 11:01 AM Wednesday. State Route 24 from milepost 15 to milepost 52 was closed Wednesday morning.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A wildfire started south of Fort Duchesne and burned through tribal land vegetation for three days before it was fully contained.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Red Bridge Fire started Sunday evening south of Fort Duchesne and burned nearly 460 acres of federal land. It was fully contained by Wednesday evening. Investigators determined the fire was human caused.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As the ridge of high pressure moved into a more favorable position near the Southern Plains, eastern Utah was able to access the deeper monsoon moisture. This led to increased coverage of showers and thunderstorms. Storms were slow moving and produced heavy rain which led to some flooding in portions of Grand County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain from thunderstorms over the Mill Creek drainage resulted in a spillover on Powerhouse Dam in Grand County. Radar estimated 0.80 to 1.10 inches of rain fell over the North Fork Mill Creek drainage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Skies cleared in the wake of a departing upper level low. This led to subfreezing temperatures across some of the southeast valleys where vegetation was susceptible to damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Overnight lows dropped to as low as 28 degrees across the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Remnants from Tropical Storm Harold lifted across the Desert Southwest. This, combined with already abundant subtropical moisture, resulted in widespread showers and thunderstorms across eastern Utah. Storms were slow moving and produced heavy rain. As a result of the heavy rain, some flash flooding occurred in portions of Grand County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 38-year-old woman was swept away in a flash flood at Grand County���s Mary Jane Canyon as a result of heavy rains occurring upstream of the canyon. 30 minute rates looked to exceed 0.40 to 0.60 inches which then flowed downstream, flooding the slot canyon. The subject reported that she had been caught in the flood and carried ���150-200 feet��� down the canyon. She stated that she heard the flood coming and tried to get to higher ground. She reached a sand bank above the creek with her dog, but the rising water eroded the sand, sending both her and the dog into the flood waters. The woman and her dog were rescued by emergency services later with no injuries reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: This snow event brought more than a foot of snow to many northern Utah valleys, with greater accumulations in the mountains.  Numerous accidents were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: State Route 210 through Little Cottonwood Canyon was closed from Tuesday night into Wednesday morning for avalanche mitigation. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another winter storm brought gusty winds and accumulating snow to much of Utah in the middle of March. Precipitation combined with snowmelt also resulted in flooding, mostly in southern Utah, including noted fatalities in a popular slot canyon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A group of hikers went on a three day trip to Buckskin Gulch and were set to return Sunday night. Family members reported them missing Monday morning when they did not return as scheduled. A search began shortly thereafter and one of the hikers was found alive Monday evening and was taken to a hospital via helicopter. The search broke off early Tuesday morning but resumed during the day Tuesday. On Tuesday evening the body of one of the other hikers was found and the third was found Wednesday afternoon. While the search was being conducted for this group of hikers, two other groups of hikers also signaled for help, bringing the total of people rescued from Buckskin Gulch to 11. The conditions were found to be treacherous in the canyon due to high water from snowmelt that has accumulated in the canyon. Given the narrow nature of the canyon in places, it takes a relatively small amount of accumulation of water to make the canyon impassable. Additionally, the location of the canyon allows water to flow into it from the south (from Arizona) as well as from the north (with origins in the vicinity of Bryce Canyon). Recent heavy rains from the ongoing atmospheric river event also hampered rescue efforts.

Even so, a stronger storm in northeast Utah produced quarter-sized hail near Maeser.

This led to the development of isolated to scattered high-based thunderstorms. Given the dry air at the lower levels, gusty outflow winds were the primary threat from storms. The stronger storms produced gusty winds in excess of 58 mph across Grand County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A peak wind gust of 63 MPH was measured by the automated weather station at the Canyonlands Field Airport outside Moab.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Spring runoff from the prior winter's record snowpack resulted in elevated flows through area rivers, and in early May, resulted in a fatality as an adult woman fell into the Weber River.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: As warmer weather set in during the spring, the prior winter's record setting snowpack began to melt, with runoff leading to cold, elevated, and fast flowing rivers accordingly. For context, the river gage at the outlet of Pineview Reservoir (which has controlled releases that subsequently flow into the Weber River downstream) rose to 7.37 feet by 5:00 pm MST on May 1, just below the historical record peak of 7.38 feet. On Monday May 1 around 4:45 pm MST, the first of many 911 emergency calls were received, stating that a person in distress was seen within the waters of the Weber River, and that they could hear screaming. First responders and emergency personnel began search and rescue operations on the Weber River from approximately Uintah to Marriott-Slaterville soon thereafter. By around 8:00 pm MST, search and rescue crews shifted to a recovery mission. Recovery crews continued for a time in the following days, but ultimately a body was never found and the missing person was presumed to have drowned. The missing person was later identified as an adult woman, age 28, and a family friend had disclosed that the woman had been walking her dogs along the Weber River at her family farm on the afternoon of the incident.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moving into the middle portion of March, the combination of ongoing snowmelt along with an atmospheric river resulted in flooding through the popular Buckskin Gulch trail. This flood resulted in numerous rescues, as well as fatalities.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A group of 3 hikers went on a three day trip through Buckskin Gulch, and were set to return Sunday night. Family members reported them missing Monday morning when they did not return as scheduled. A search began shortly thereafter with one of the hikers found alive on Monday, and taken to a hospital via helicopter. The body of a deceased hiker was found late in the day on Monday.  A second deceased individual was discovered on Wednesday, 3 miles south of the Utah/Arizona border on a patch of land in the middle of the Paria River. It is presumed this hiker suffered fatal injuries while within Buckskin Gulch in Utah, and was subsequently washed downstream into the Paria River. During the search and rescue operations, two other groups of hikers signaling for help were rescued, bringing the total number of people rescued from Buckskin Gulch to 11. ||The conditions were found to be treacherous in the canyon due to high water from rain and snowmelt. Given the narrow nature of the canyon in places, it takes a relatively small amount of water to make the canyon impassable. Additionally, the location of the canyon allows water to flow into it from the south (from Arizona) as well as from the north (with origins in the vicinity of Bryce Canyon).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A skier unintentionally triggered an avalanche which resulted in a partial burial and leg injury.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two backcountry skiers were skiing in the Butler Fork area of Big Cottonwood Canyon during the day. The terrain is noted to be a northeast facing bowl, with slope angle of about 38 degrees, and elevation of around 9000 feet. A fracture in the snow occurred approximately 50 feet above them. While one skier was on the periphery of the ensuing slide, the other was caught in the middle, pushed into a stand of trees, and buried up to their chest. During this time, the skier suffered an apparent leg/hamstring injury. The injured skier was subsequently dug out by their partner. No exact time is given, so event time is estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A natural avalanche near Bridal Veil Falls in Provo Canyon resulted in road impacts, a brief damming of a nearby river, and one subsequent indirect injury.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large naturally occurring avalanche took place in the morning hours near Bridal Veil Falls in Provo Canyon. Snow with the avalanche flow covered the entire base of the falls in Provo Canyon and deposited snow onto State Route 189. According to the Utah Highway Patrol, trees were included within the flow, resulting in blocking and damming of the Provo River for a period of time. Several hours after the avalanche, an employee with the water conservancy district was inspecting a flooded building, at which time a portion of the snow dam holding back the river current broke free. The employee was caught in the floodwater to an extent, managed to cling to a tree until water began to recede, and then was able to find safe ground on a cement stack in the middle of the river until rescue crews could get to him. The employee was taken to a nearby hospital to be treated for hypothermia. Per the Utah Avalanche Center, avalanche danger was rated in the high category along all slopes and elevations in the Provo area for the day.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After high pressure dominated eastern Utah for a couple of days, moisture gradually increased ahead of an approaching system. This led to the development of isolated to scattered high-based thunderstorms. Given the dry air at the lower levels, gusty outflow winds were the primary threat from storms. The stronger storms produced gusty winds in excess of 58 mph across Uintah County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A peak wind gust of 68 MPH was measured by the automated weather station at the Vernal Regional Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another in a series of winter storms impacted the region early to mid March, resulting in widespread snow accumulation primarily throughout Utah's high terrain. High winds were observed in many locations. An avalanche related fatality is also noted within the time period of this storm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A group including two guides, eleven clients, and two snowcat operators were in the Western Uinta Mountains for backcountry skiing. For their final run, the group ascended to the top of a point in the Upper Weber Canyon approximately 10400 feet in elevation with a southeast aspect and slope angle of around 37 degrees. After one guide and two skiers descended, a third skier began descending and fell part way down. A fourth skier began their descent, with the avalanche subsequently releasing at approximately 3:21 pm. The avalanche caught the third and fourth skier, carrying them downhill and fully burying them. A guide called for medical and search and rescue aid, and began searching for the buried skiers. One skier was found buried around 5 feet deep, but was breathing and alert when uncovered. The skier was fully excavated and then transferred via air ambulance to a local hospital. Some time later, the transceiver signal for the second buried skier was located. The skier was found buried upside down around 6 feet deep, not breathing, and with no pulse. The skier was pronounced dead approximately 1 hour and 20 minutes after the avalanche.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A brush fire was reported around 10:15 a.m. along Interstate 15 near Leeds. Wind gusts at an automated weather station near Leeds were from the northeast at around 45 mph. The northbound lanes of Interstate 15 were closed around 10:30 a.m. Lanes were opened shortly after 12:00 p.m. The fire damaged power poles that caused local power outages.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Clearing skies and a much colder airmass behind a departing low pressure system led to sub-freezing temperatures across all of eastern Utah, including low-elevation valleys.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Overnight lows ranged from 17 to 29 degrees F across the zone, including a low of 17 degrees F at Blanding Municipal Airport. This constituted a season-ending freeze, as all reporting sites dropped below 28 degrees F for 3 hours or more.

CZ_NAME: GREAT SALT LAKE DESERT AND MOUNTAINS
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another winter storm brought accumulating snow, primarily to northern Utah, though limited warning criteria amounts were reported. Additionally, a second avalanche fatality for the month is noted.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At some time between approximately 5:30 pm to 6:15 pm Monday 3/27, a large avalanche in the Pole Canyon area of the southern Oquirrh Mountains resulted in the full burial of a snowmobiler. A second snowmobiler who was not buried was able to call for emergency services, and search and rescue began soon thereafter for the buried snowmobiler. The buried snowmobiler was equipped with a beacon, and by 8:48 pm the body of the deceased 38-year-old man was recovered under approximately 13.2 feet of snow. The buried avalanche victim had deployed an avalanche airbag, but the airbag was deflated when the victim was found. This large avalanche was reported to have a fracture line 4250 feet wide, traveling a horizontal distance of 1.26 miles (2360 vertical feet downhill).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unseasonably strong cold front moved through northeast Utah and western Colorado, bringing moderate to heavy snowfall to the northern and central mountains with highest accumulations across the central mountains due to banded precipitation. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 5 to 12 inches of snow fell across the area with 10 to 12 inches on the eastern side and 5 to 7 inches on the western side. Locally higher amount up to 13 inches estimated at Kings Cabin SNOTEL site.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An avalanche occurred near the Gold Ridge / Hardscrabble backcountry area of Morgan County, resulting in the burial of a snowcat and its occupants.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At around 11:30 am, a large avalanche occurred near the Gold Ridge / Hardscrabble backcountry area, approximately 7 miles ESE of Farmington. At the time of the avalanche, one snowcat carrying two occupants was caught in the slide and buried. A second snowcat and its five occupants were able to quickly uncover one of the buried snowcat occupants, who had sustained minor head injuries. This occupant was subsequently taken to an area hospital by ambulance. The second occupant was buried with their leg trapped beneath the snowcat, but was uncovered enough that they could breath. When rescue crews arrived, they were able to uncover the buried occupant, who was then transported by helicopter to a nearby hospital with a serious leg injury.

Some of these storms were strong, yielding damaging wind gusts and hail. Additionally, a road washout was reported as a result of continued spring runoff from the prior winter's record snowfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Utah County Sherriff's Department initially reported a partial washout of the Nebo Loop Scenic Byway in Payson Canyon near the Shram Creek Trailhead on 5/22/23 associated with ongoing spring snowmelt runoff (likely further exacerbated by nearly daily scattered afternoon showers and thunderstorms). By 5/25/23, pictures and follow-up reports showed that the road had fully washed out due to the continued runoff.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High flows on the Ogden River associated with snowmelt from the record winter resulted in a portion of state Route 39 in Ogden Canyon being washed out.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At approximately 0830 MST on Thursday, May 11, 2023, high flows on the Lower Ogden River eroded the embankment supporting state Route 39 through Ogden Canyon which caused one lane of the two lane road to collapse. An upstream river gauge reported flows above action stage (greater than 1264 cfs) from April 14 through May 2, and again from May 4 through the time of the incident. Flows approached minor flood stage (1610 cfs) within 100 cfs throughout this time, but exceeded minor flood stage by 5 cfs at one observation time on May 9, 2023. The road was closed for approximately 11 hours, with the one stable lane allowed to reopen thereafter.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Sufficient moisture continued to flow through the region in late July, leading to daytime convection most days. This convection was occasionally strong to severe, yielding localized strong wind gusts in addition to flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain and thunderstorms impacted the Halfway Hill Fire burn scar area during the afternoon hours. Subsequently, the Millard County Emergency Manager reported that there were heavy flows out of Copleys Canyon within the burn scar, with flows and debris across the main canyon road and into a campground. This required rock and debris removal.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The ongoing active pattern lessened somewhat, but enough moisture and support remained to trigger isolated to scattered showers and thunderstorms which resulted in a few instances of strong wind gusts and flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Capitol Reef National Park reported flash flooding associated with thunderstorms in the area, which necessitated the closure of several roads in the park, including portions of the Scenic Drive, The Capitol Gorge Road, and Pleasant Creek.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A brief resurgence of the active pattern resulted in scattered strong thunderstorms which resulted in reports of a landspout, hail, and flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Capitol Reef National Park officials reported a debris flow across state Route 24, making it impassable just east of the Visitor Center between Hickman Bridge and Grand Wash. Additionally, flash flooding was observed in Sulphur Creek, and high flows were reported in Grand Wash. This flooding was associated with a cluster of showers and thunderstorms which produced heavy rain over portions of the park, with Multi-Radar Multi-Sensor  rainfall estimates of approximately 0.75 to 1.25.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong winter storm brought widespread snowfall and winter impacts to much of the forecast region for the start of 2023. Some of the high elevation locations saw snow begin prior to the new year, while most valley locations saw rain switch to snow right around midnight on the new year. Additionally, isolated flash flooding was reported in southern Utah due to rainfall associated with the system.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several SNOTEL stations including Rees Flat, Beaver Dams, and Red Pine Ridge recorded 1.8 to 4.7 inches of snow water equivalent, resulting in approximately 17 to 44 inches of snow. Note that some snow in the high elevations began as early as 12/30/22.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep monsoonal event impacted Utah at the beginning of August. Each of these days across northern Utah, widespread thunderstorms and flooding impacted the region. Very heavy rainfall impacted West Valley City, Honeyville, and Draper in particular.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple streets and Hunter High School in West Valley City were flooded. Flooding led to vehicles stranded along California Avenue between 2700 West and Gladiola Street in Salt Lake City.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong winter storm brought widespread snowfall and winter impacts to much of the forecast region for the start of 2023. Some of the high elevation locations saw snow begin prior to the new year, while most valley locations saw rain switch to snow right around midnight on the new year. Additionally, isolated flash flooding was reported in southern Utah due to rainfall associated with the system.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Wasatch Mountains South of I-80 zone saw some of the highest snowfall totals through the event. Several SNOTEL and snow safety community observations including those at Solitude Mountain Resort, Snowbird, Alta Ski Area, and Brighton reported between 2.6 to 4.8 inches of snow water equivalent, resulting in approximately 31 to 44 inches of snow. The highest reported snowfall total was 52 inches at Sundance Mountain Resort. In addition, it was reported that at least a dozen 50 foot trees were felled near Sundance Mountain Resort due to the heavy snow, bringing down power lines in the area. Note that some snow in the high elevations began as early as 12/30/22.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep monsoonal event impacted Utah at the beginning of August. Each of these days across northern Utah, widespread thunderstorms and flooding impacted the region. Very heavy rainfall impacted West Valley City, Honeyville, and Draper in particular.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Late on August 3, the city of Draper declared a state of emergency as flash flooding and mudslides led to road closures on Highland Drive, Bangerter Parkway, and Minuteman Drive. A portion of Bangerter Parkway, near 14200 South, was damaged on the uphill lanes. A number of homes sustained water damage. According to the City of Draper, there were 428 residential damage report forms submitted, with estimated damages of $11,255,765. There were 11 business damage report forms submitted, with estimated damages of $844,235. City infrastructure estimated damage totaled $4,000,000.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weather system moving through Utah brought widespread precipitation in addition to isolated flash flooding and strong wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Fairly persistent showers moved over Sulphur Creek during the morning hours, with thunderstorms moving over the Capitol Reef National Park area during the early afternoon. The storms resulted in quick bursts of heavy rain, with radar estimates showing around 0.4 to 0.6 inches of rainfall over the affected area. Subsequently, Capitol Reef National Park officials reported approximately 8 to 10 inches of water flowing through Sulphur Creek near the Capitol Reef National Park Visitor Center. A visitor report of knee- to thigh-deep water within Spring Canyon was also relayed from a Park Ranger.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms across the region resulted in hazardous conditions developing in area slot canyons and typically dry washes. The Buckskin Gulch slot canyon had a flood wave develop, with subsequent fatalities associated with the flood.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms moved across the area from late morning through the afternoon hours. Radar estimates indicated 1.25 inches to 2.5 inches of total rainfall near the affected area. A Park Ranger with the Grand Staircase-Escalante National Monument subsequently reported and sent pictures of Hole-in-the-Rock road near the 25 Mile Wash area, where floodwaters associated with the rain overtopped road culverts and resulted in a minor road washout.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms across the region resulted in hazardous conditions developing in area slot canyons and typically dry washes. The Buckskin Gulch slot canyon had a flood wave develop, with subsequent fatalities associated with the flood.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed across southern Utah on Saturday, moving across the Grand Staircase-Escalante National Monument towards the Glen Canyon National Recreation Area. Locally heavy rainfall was associated with these thunderstorms, with radar estimated rainfall from around 0.75 inches to 1.5 inches noted across the Buckskin Gulch area. Two flood waves resulted, as noted on the Paria River at Lees Ferry gauge, downstream of the canyon. This gauge showed an initial gradual increase to around 50 cfs over the course of 2 hours, followed by a rapid increase to approximately 150 cfs over 15 minutes. The second bump would correlate to roughly a 1 foot rise at Lees Ferry, which would be much higher in the narrow slot canyon area of Buckskin Gulch. During the time of flood waves propagation through the slot canyon, at least 2 permitted hikers were hiking through the canyon. They were reported to be planning on hiking approximately 5 miles into Wire Pass and the Buckskin Gulch slot canyon areas. Kane County Sherriff's department received word of a deceased person within the trail, and subsequently recovered the bodies of two deceased individuals. It is believed that the men drowned in the initial flood wave, and were then washed 7 to 10 miles downstream.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A brief resurgence of the active pattern resulted in scattered strong thunderstorms which resulted in reports of a landspout, hail, and flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding was reported on Short Creek and its tributaries through the Hilldale and Colorado City area. All creek road crossings were closed for 2 to 3 hours, and the Hilldale Street Creek crossing was heavily damaged and subsequently closed indefinitely until repairs could be made. This flooding is the result of strong thunderstorms which per Multi-Radar Multi-Sensor, produced approximately 3.00 to 4.00 of rainfall, largely within the span of 2 hours, though some of this can likely be attributed to hail contamination. At 6:25 am MST on the 14th, the downstream Fort Pearce Wash gauge recorded a spike in flow to 124 CFS associated with the flood water propagation.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A woman was killed hiking the Fairyland Loop Trail because of flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 64-year-old woman was reported overdue from a hike on the Fairyland Loop Trail. A search of the area was conducted, with the woman being found deceased. Details are unclear, but showers and thunderstorms were in the area. A news release from Bryce Canyon National Park stated dry washes had higher flows because of precipitation.  Though not conclusive, a medical examiner report stated the cause of death was likely due to drowning.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An active pattern resulted in strong wind gusts in addition to isolated flash flooding to start the month of September.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: As a result of heavy rain in the area, the East Fork Virgin - Springdale, guage recorded a  rapid rise in flow from its initial general flow level of around 68 CFS. An initial peak of 1228 CFS was observed at approximately 4:00 pm MST. The river then fell to 359 CFS at approximately 7:00 pm MST, before rising again to a secondary peak of 858 CFS at approximately 11:00 pm MST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An influx of moisture restarted a brief active period moving towards the middle of September. As a result, the region saw a couple of days with scattered showers and thunderstorms, some of which produced strong gusty winds, with one producing a landspout.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: LANDSPOUT. Location: 41.95, -112.07. Broadcast media shared a public video of a landspout northwest of Clarkston, UT in an open field. No damage was reported with this landspout. The observer who relayed the report and video to broadcast media reported the time to be approximately 3:30 pm MST. Radar imagery around that time did show a thunderstorm in the area with weak and broad rotation along an associated outflow boundary.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: From the afternoon of July 6 on into the evening, isolated to scattered clusters of showers and thunderstorms impacted portions of northern Utah and southwest Wyoming, yielding a few strong to severe wind gusts.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A brief resurgence of the active pattern resulted in scattered strong thunderstorms which resulted in reports of a landspout, hail, and flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Grand Staircase-Escalante National Monument Big Water Visitor Center reported a flash flood wave along Wahweap Creek that was audible from the town of Big Water until around 8 pm MST. A survey of the creek at the Smokey Mountain Road low-water crossing the following morning revealed the flood wave was approximately 60 to 75 feet wide at some point overnight, which would roughly equate to around 3 to 4 feet of depth at this particular point. This flooding was associated with heavy rain that fell within the far headwaters from approximately 3 pm MST to 5 pm MST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A brief resurgence of the active pattern resulted in scattered strong thunderstorms which resulted in reports of a landspout, hail, and flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Grand Staircase-Escalante National Monument Kanab Visitor Center officials reported flash flooding in the town, with local streets flooded and covered with mud and debris, resulting in closure of a few streets. Creeks through town were overflowing across roads at culverts, and in one spot, Kanab Creek was overflowing across the road with water approximately 9 to 12 deep. Dirt roads on the outskirts of town were impassable. Additionally, Kane County Sheriff's office officials reported that several homes had some minor basement flooding. This flooding is the result of strong thunderstorms in the area, with Multi-Radar Multi-Sensor estimates of approximately 0.75 to 1.50 of rainfall in around 3 hours.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorms and associated outflow winds moved through the area during the early evening. At approximately 6:18 pm MST, a witness saw a tree fall onto a 68-year-old bicyclist riding along the Denver and Rio Grande Western Rail paved multiuse trail. Law enforcement arrived on scene, finding the woman unconscious and pinned beneath the felled 40 to 50 ft tree. After being removed, life saving measures were preformed and she was transported to a local hospital in critical condition, where she later died from her injuries. The nearest observation in the area is from Hill AFB, which measured a gust of 67 mph at 6:26 pm MST. Other supporting wind gusts are noted from these strong storms in the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another winter storm brought gusty winds and accumulating snow to much of Utah in the middle of March. Precipitation combined with snowmelt also resulted in flooding, mostly in southern Utah, including noted fatalities in a popular slot canyon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An initial peak wind gust of 67 mph was recorded at the US-6 @ Eureka mesonet site at 6:50 pm 3/14, with subsequent gusts between 58 mph and 61 mph noted thereafter. Additionally, a wind gust of 58 mph was recorded at the Lake Mountain mesonet site at 4:40 am 3/15.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another winter storm brought gusty winds and accumulating snow to much of Utah in the middle of March. Precipitation combined with snowmelt also resulted in flooding, mostly in southern Utah, including noted fatalities in a popular slot canyon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Broad areas of sheet flooding were observed in the greater Tremonton area due to rain on snow. Roads in communities near the Sky Way Golf Course in Tremonton were noted to be under several inches of water, with several homes impacted. Water was also reported to be overflowing the banks of the Malad River and Bear River.
